This lesson is all about creating a compelling entry point into your world using a 500-800 word Facebook ad. Start by watching this one-hour presentation. Sean explains how to think about Facebook ads from a perspective we've never shared publicly before. Then he discusses a way to create an authentic, conversational Facebook ad that doesn't feel like an ad. Two things before you watch the video. First, Sean mentions the importance of the hook in a Facebook ad early and he forgot to mention it again. That's one of the dangers of recording these videos in a single take. You'll find the relevant information about the importance of a Facebook hook and how to create them, excerpted from the traffic engine, below. Second, a summary of your homework appears below as well. We chose selections to review and share from the work submitted. Facebook ad hooks, excerpted from the traffic engine. The hook is the first two lines of a Facebook ad. That's my term, I've heard that called by many names. My two favorite hooks are dialogue and questions, which can be combined too. You are never going to believe what my accountant told me about the payroll protection loan. I'm sorry Sean, I talked to your attorney and there's nothing we can do to help. What if everything I believed about gaining muscle after turning 40 was wrong? Three things I wish every digital marketer knew about paid advertising on Google and Facebook. The opportunities are endless. The first two lines of an ad pre-qualify, so be careful about who and how you attract. At the same time, remember that the first two lines sell the rest of the ad. A little conversational curiosity to get a prospect's attention is okay, in my opinion, if the ad copy is transparent. Sean's Rule of Three. The Rule of Three was first mentioned during a landing page review. Sean realized he hadn't ever mentioned it to anyone, including Andre. The Rule of Three can be used for everything you'll write in this workshop. It works at the micro level, for example, what to include in a single Facebook ad, and at the macro level, for example, your thematic arc might follow the Rule of Three. Here's an overview. Enter Sean. BTW, I'm sure I did not invent this idea, I just gave the framework a name when I noticed how often I use it. Structure is important to me when I write. I want to know where I'm going and the order in which I'll get there. I also want to know that my ideas flow from one to the next, that I've supported those ideas well, and that there's a natural progression from A to B to C. The Rule of Three starts with a controlling idea, thesis statement, this is the main point I'm making. Then, I make three main points that are each accompanied by three supporting ideas. Finally, I want to direct the reader, or viewer, to what's next. Structurally, I imagine it like this. Controlling idea, thesis statement. Main point number one, supporting idea number one, supporting idea number two, supporting idea number three, main point number two, supporting idea number one, supporting idea number two, supporting idea number three, main point number three, supporting idea number one, Supporting idea number 2 Supporting idea number 3 Call to action This framework works for writing and editing. For example, if you're writing a 1000 word Facebook ad, start with the main takeaway you want the reader to believe, that's your controlling idea. Then, identify the three things that person needs to believe in order to accept your controlling idea. Make sure those ideas make sense and that they follow logically from one to the other. They're not just random ideas thrown on the page. Next, support each of those three main points with, up to, three proof points. Finally, after you've made your case, like a prosecutor, tell the reader what to do next. If you're editing your work or another person's work, map their ideas to the rule of three structure. This can be an eye-opening exercise. Is the controlling idea obvious? Do the main points flow? Are they supported with evidence? Does the CTA lead naturally to what's next? I suspect some of you are wondering does it have to be three main points and three supporting ideas? No. These are guidelines. However, three by three works very well in most circumstances. Judgment and discretion are required. And yes, for those of you who are a student observers, the rule of three is fractal. There are three parts, controlling idea, making the case, call to action, and within each part are three additional parts. If you're writing much longer copy, you might include three sub points under each supporting idea, for example. It's threes within threes within threes. Our brains seem to understand groups of three well. The key to this framework's effectiveness is its simplicity. Don't overthink it. Lesson 3 homework. Write your own 500-800 word Facebook at following the guidelines from the screencast. This ad is the entry point for prospects into your world, and it has three jobs. Note the frame for the entire customer journey. Attract prospects who can become happy customers. Repel prospects who are unlikely to become happy customers. Do not overthink this exercise. You are not writing prose, and you shouldn't be thinking about writing ad copy. We want to avoid any tone that feels like copy, advertising. Write conversationally. Instead, we're writing from the heart by focusing on the needs of our audience first, then setting them up for a journey into our world through the lens of the frame we've established. Three suggestions to keep Facebook happy, and improve your results. Do no use you, your, or similar language that calls out a person as an individual. Do not use numbers, especially as proof points. This can be really tricky because you may want to include statements like I've worked with hundreds of people who use your discretion, just make sure you avoid making any outcome-based claims, in general. For example, even if it's 100% true I would not say I've helped hundreds of people grow their revenue, on average, 65%. Avoiding bold claims altogether is safer with Facebook. Shorter sentences and shorter paragraphs are important. More white spaces make sure writing easier to read. Note, in the feedback recording below we refer to this as Week 2, not Lesson 3. As we said at the start, we've retooled the live workshop to now be a self-paced course. Week 2, Lesson 3 feedback. We ended up reviewing four homework submissions that we felt would be broadly valuable to everyone, their ads are in the comments below. Shane Mulanson. Stacy. Francis Waldekker. Nick Warren. We evaluated the ads, we used the following questions, we encourage you to use these questions yourself. Facebook ad review questions. Do the first two sentences grab the prospect's attention and make him or her want to read more? Is the copy communicate empathy and understanding by focusing first on the prospect's needs, desires, fears and concerns? Is there a rising and falling of tension that pulls the reader's attention through the ad from beginning to end? Are the benefits from engaging with the writing obvious to the reader? Is the call to action interesting, compelling, and intriguing? Here is our 2-hour 8-minute review of the work. Post your questions below. Andre and Sean.
